title: Google Teachable Machine
description:
Incredibly easy to start using it. Set up takes less than a minute. Possible activities to use this tool with include:
  • Use different inputs/number of image samples (many samples vs few samples) and see how this affects the model.
  • Change the parameters (epochs, batch size, learning rate) to see how it affects the test results. Look at the accuracy/loss and plot them.
  • Check for biases/imbalances in the data set and attempt to correct it. Example: Class 1 is 'highlighters', but only yellow ones are used in training, so the model might fail to recognize other colors.
  • Add noise to the input images/audio to see how it affects results.
It isn't very technically difficult and doesn't require a lot of background knowledge, but seems like a very useful tool to introduce the topic. Also, lots of activities can be created and implemented. I attached an Image Project .tm file that can be opened using the website linked in the title of this card. To open: click the link, then click on the hamburger menu on the top left of the site, then select 'Open project from file'.

title: Introduction to AI-Assisted Coding
file: files/AI Assisted Code/AI Assisted Coding Assignment.zip
description: I found this activity in the Hour of AI website, but couldn't get the Online IDE to work, so I recreated the assignment (see 'Introduction to AI Assisted Coding Assignment' file) so it doesn't need the website at all, but it is linked in the title anyways for reference.

This could be useful for learning about prompt engineering and fixing/analysing code. Maybe only letting the students prompt the AI once and then they have to fix the rest themselves. This could also be done with other games like NYT - Wordle. I did two prompts for comparison, one simple prompt, and one detailed prompt, both had their issues. I provided the prompts and the results under 'Prompt Engineering Comparison' and I included the code under the Detailed and Simple Prompt folders.
I think this is good for beginners or more advanced students depending on the activity requirements, but it can also can teach students about web dev.

title: Interactive Transformer Visualization of GPT-2
description: This tool generates one word at a time and visualizes the steps it takes to guess the next word. Using the tool is very easy, the website includes a bunch of text at the bottom explaining what the transformer is doing.
The concepts are easy to understand, but it is just a bunch of text, so maybe turning it into slides and letting students create sentences would be a good class activity.

The lecture part is what would take the longest, but the actual tool shouldn't take very long. It also only has one parameter: Temperature which 'Changes the output probability distribution and randomness of next token. '
